narration.default: Sometimes one calculation has several answers — a sum AND a product, a name AND an age, x AND y coordinates. Until now, our functions could only return ONE thing. Today you'll learn to return TWO at the same time, then unpack them on the receiving side. Tiny addition to what you already know about return.

steps.title: Define a calculator that returns two values

narration.default:
Type:

def calculate(a, b):
    return a + b, a * b

Look at the return — there are TWO values separated by a comma. Python wraps them up together as a pair. The function still hands ONE thing back; that one thing just happens to contain both values. We'll see how to take them apart on the next line.

Below, type:

sum_result, product_result = calculate(5, 3)

The two names on the left, separated by a comma, are unpacking. Python takes the pair returned by calculate and puts the first value into sum_result, the second into product_result. This is called tuple unpacking — and it works any time a function returns multiple values.

Type:

print("Sum:", sum_result)
print("Product:", product_result)

Now each value is a normal variable you can use independently. The function did one job — both calculations on the same two numbers — and handed back both results in one trip.
Click Run. The terminal prints Sum: 8 and Product: 15. One call, two answers, both useful. Try changing the numbers — calculate(7, 7) gives 14 and 49.

Real uses: a function that splits a full name into first and last; a function that returns the min AND max of a list; a function that returns the x AND y of a sprite's position. Returning multiple values keeps related results together. The pair Python builds on the right of return is a tuple — the same fixed-together pack you met in the Tuples tutorial.
